questions.stem: The price elasticity of demand (PED) measures which of the following?
options: The change in income of consumers
The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in the good's own price
The slope of the supply curve
questions.explanation: PED measures how responsive the quantity demanded of a good is to a change in its own price, defined as the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The formula for the price elasticity of demand is which of the following?
options: Change in quantity demanded times change in price
Percentage change in price divided by percentage change in quantity demanded
Percentage change in quantity demanded divided by percentage change in price
questions.explanation: PED is the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price, $\text{PED} = \%\Delta Q_d / \%\Delta P$. Because demand slopes down, the raw value is usually negative and often quoted as an absolute value.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Demand is described as price inelastic when the absolute value of the price elasticity of demand is which of the following?
questions.explanation: When demand is inelastic, the magnitude of PED is less than 1, so quantity demanded changes proportionately less than price. If the magnitude exceeds 1 demand is elastic, and a magnitude of exactly 1 is unit elastic.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When the price of a good rises from 10 to 12 (a 20 percent rise) and quantity demanded falls from 100 to 90 (a 10 percent fall), the price elasticity of demand has a magnitude of:
questions.explanation: PED magnitude $= \%\Delta Q_d / \%\Delta P = 10\% / 20\% = 0.5$. Since this is less than 1, demand is inelastic over this range.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When the price of a good falls from 5 to 4 (a 20 percent fall) and quantity demanded rises from 200 to 260 (a 30 percent rise), the price elasticity of demand has a magnitude of:
questions.explanation: PED magnitude $= 30\% / 20\% = 1.5$. Because this exceeds 1, demand is elastic: quantity responds proportionately more than price.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If demand for a good is price inelastic, a rise in its price will do what to the total revenue of sellers?
options: Total revenue stays exactly the same
questions.explanation: With inelastic demand, quantity falls proportionately less than price rises, so total revenue (price times quantity) increases. For example, raising price from 10 to 12 while quantity falls from 100 to 90 lifts revenue from 1000 to 1080.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If demand for a good is price elastic, a rise in its price will do what to total revenue?
questions.explanation: With elastic demand, quantity falls proportionately more than price rises, so the loss of sales outweighs the higher price and total revenue falls. To raise revenue with elastic demand, a seller should cut price, not raise it.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When demand has unit price elasticity (magnitude exactly 1), a change in price will do what to total revenue?
questions.explanation: Unit elasticity means quantity changes by the same percentage as price, in the opposite direction, so the two effects cancel and total revenue stays constant as price changes.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A perfectly inelastic demand curve has which shape and elasticity value?
options: Upward sloping, with elasticity equal to zero
Horizontal, with elasticity equal to infinity
Vertical, with elasticity equal to zero
Downward sloping, with elasticity equal to 1
questions.explanation: Perfectly inelastic demand means quantity demanded does not change at all when price changes, so PED is zero and the curve is vertical. Buyers purchase the same amount whatever the price.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A perfectly elastic demand curve has which shape and elasticity value?
options: Steeply downward sloping, with elasticity equal to 1
Horizontal, with elasticity equal to infinity
Vertical, with elasticity equal to zero
Upward sloping, with elasticity equal to infinity
questions.explanation: Perfectly elastic demand means buyers will take any amount at a given price but nothing at all above it, so the curve is horizontal and PED is infinite. This is the demand facing a single firm in perfect competition.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following goods is most likely to have price inelastic demand?
options: One restaurant among many in a city
A particular brand of soft drink among many
A basic necessity such as salt with few substitutes
questions.explanation: Necessities with few close substitutes, such as salt, tend to have inelastic demand: buyers keep purchasing roughly the same amount even if price changes. Goods with many substitutes or that take a large share of income tend to be elastic.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is a key determinant making the demand for a good more price elastic?
options: The good has many close substitutes
The good takes up a tiny fraction of income
The time period considered is very short
The good is a basic necessity
questions.explanation: The availability of close substitutes is the most important determinant of elasticity. If good substitutes exist, buyers switch readily when price rises, making demand elastic. Necessities, small budget shares, and short time periods tend to make demand inelastic.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Other things equal, demand for a good tends to become more elastic when which of the following is true?
options: More time is available to adjust to the price change
Less time is available to adjust
questions.explanation: Given more time, consumers can find substitutes and change habits, so demand becomes more elastic in the long run than in the short run. Short adjustment time, no substitutes, and addiction all tend to make demand inelastic.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The income elasticity of demand measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in which variable?
options: The price of a related good
questions.explanation: Income elasticity of demand is the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in income. Its sign tells us whether a good is normal (positive) or inferior (negative).
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A good with a negative income elasticity of demand is classified as which of the following?
questions.explanation: Negative income elasticity means quantity demanded falls as income rises, which is the defining feature of an inferior good. Normal goods have positive income elasticity.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When income rises by 20 percent and the quantity demanded of a good rises by 20 percent, the income elasticity of demand is:
options: $0$, demand does not respond to income
questions.explanation: Income elasticity $= \%\Delta Q_d / \%\Delta\text{income} = 20\% / 20\% = +1.0$. The positive sign means the good is normal, and a value of 1 means demand rises in step with income.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A normal good with an income elasticity of demand greater than 1 is often described as which of the following?
questions.explanation: When income elasticity exceeds 1, demand rises proportionately more than income, so spending on the good takes up a larger share of income as people get richer. Such goods are called luxuries or income elastic goods.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Cross elasticity of demand between two goods measures the responsiveness of the quantity demanded of one good to a change in which of the following?
options: The price of the other good
questions.explanation: Cross elasticity of demand is the percentage change in the quantity demanded of good A divided by the percentage change in the price of good B. Its sign reveals whether the goods are substitutes or complements.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A positive cross elasticity of demand between good A and good B indicates that the two goods are which of the following?
questions.explanation: A positive cross elasticity means a rise in the price of B increases demand for A, which is the behaviour of substitutes such as tea and coffee. Complements have a negative cross elasticity.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A negative cross elasticity of demand between two goods indicates that they are which of the following?
questions.explanation: A negative cross elasticity means a rise in the price of one good reduces demand for the other, which is true of complements such as cars and petrol that are used together.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Along a straight-line (linear) downward sloping demand curve, the price elasticity of demand does which of the following as you move down the curve?
options: Is always equal to the slope
Falls in magnitude from elastic to inelastic
questions.explanation: On a straight-line demand curve the slope is constant but elasticity is not. Elasticity is large (elastic) near the top where price is high and quantity low, equals 1 at the midpoint, and is small (inelastic) near the bottom.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A government wanting to raise tax revenue by taxing a good will collect the most revenue, other things equal, by taxing a good whose demand is which of the following?
questions.explanation: When demand is inelastic, a tax that raises the price reduces quantity only slightly, so sales hold up and the tax yields more revenue. This is why taxes are often placed on goods such as tobacco and fuel.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Price elasticity of supply measures which of the following?
options: The responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price
The responsiveness of quantity demanded to income
The slope of the demand curve
questions.explanation: Price elasticity of supply is the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price. It tells us how readily producers expand output when the price rises.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Supply of a good is generally more elastic in the long run than in the short run mainly because:
options: Firms have more time to adjust capacity and inputs
Prices never change in the long run
questions.explanation: Given enough time, firms can build new plant, hire and train workers, and enter or leave the industry. This greater flexibility makes the quantity supplied more responsive to price, so supply is more elastic in the long run.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When the price of a good rises from 20 to 16 (a 20 percent fall) the quantity demanded rises from 80 to 100 (a 25 percent rise). Demand over this range is best described as:
questions.explanation: PED magnitude $= 25\% / 20\% = 1.25$, which is greater than 1, so demand is elastic. Quantity responds proportionately more than price over this range.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A firm selling a good with elastic demand wants to increase its total revenue. According to elasticity theory, it should do which of the following?
questions.explanation: With elastic demand, a price cut raises quantity demanded proportionately more than the fall in price, so total revenue increases. Lowering price is the way to raise revenue when demand is elastic.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
